value.Title: Here's what you want us to ask Acton and Ramaswamy Ohio voters want the candidates for governor to provide clear stances on key issues like energy, education, taxes, healthcare and voting rights.

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JGY-SMN3-SJJB-4491-00000-00Here's what you want us to ask Acton and Ramaswamy Ohio voters want the candidates for governor to provide clear stances on key issues like energy, education, taxes, healthcare and voting rights. 2026-05-04T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:08:35ZLexisNexisAPg. 001Here's what you want us to ask Acton and Ramaswamy Ohio voters want the candidates for governor to provide clear stances on key issues like energy, education, taxes, healthcare and voting rights. Chris Quinn - cquinn@cleveland.com and Advance Local Express Desk

With seven months until Election Day, Ohio's race for governor is already filling televisions and phones with attack ads, slogans and broad promises.

But if the hundreds of responses to a text message I sent last week is an indication, voters are hungry for something far more basic: clear positions on the issues that will shape their lives.

More than 300 readers responded after I sent subscribers a text Friday asking what questions cleveland.com and The Plain Dealer should press the candidates to answer. The race is shaping up around Republican Vivek Ramaswamy and Democrat Amy Acton.

The message from readers was notable for its consistency. While responses came from across the political spectrum, readers have broad agreement that Ohioans deserve specifics on taxes, education, energy, voting rights, healthcare and whether state leaders will respect the will of voters.

Many readers also said they are weary of negative advertising and campaign noise. They want debates, side-by-side comparisons and direct answers that can be judged on merit.

Subscribers can join these weekday conversations for free at joinsubtext.com/chrisquinn, where readers help shape coverage, suggest stories and weigh in on issues driving Ohio.

This is what I sent:

Amy Acton and Vivek Ramaswamy are hitting us with ads in Ohio's race for governor, but other than broad strokes, do you have any idea where they stand on key issues? We're seven months from Election Day. Isn't it time to know their positions on subsidizing data centers with tax dollars, prohibiting local governments from zoning rules on nuclear power plants, taxpayer-funded school vouchers, daycare subsidies, the income tax vs property tax debate, actual strategies for strengthening higher education, capital punishment, hemp beverage legalization, redistricting methods and more? Let's demand answers. What else should be on the list?

Here, based on volume and repetition, are the issues readers most want candidates to talk about, and now.

$Energy, environment and local control

No topic generated more consensus than Ohio's energy future.

Readers repeatedly asked where candidates stand on solar and wind power, nuclear plants, fracking under state parks, grid modernization, algae blooms in Lake Erie, water quality and whether local communities should control what gets built in their backyards.

Many sharply opposed a proposal by state lawmakers to strip municipalities of zoning authority over nuclear facilities or energy projects.

"Local governments and citizens want the power to control locations of where data centers and power plants will be built."

"Support for renewable energy and grid modernization."

"Cleaning up our lakes and rivers, improving air quality, decreasing algae blooms in Lake Erie."

Several also wanted answers on subsidizing data centers and whether such projects should be required to provide their own electricity rather than strain the public grid.

$Education, vouchers and the cost of schools

The second-largest cluster of responses centered on education -- from kindergarten through college.

Readers want to know whether candidates support taxpayer-funded vouchers for private schools, what accountability private schools should face when receiving public money, and how Ohio will fairly fund public schools.

Many also raised concerns over rising property taxes tied to school levies.

"Schools that receive voucher money have to meet the same standards as public schools."

"Why should taxpayers subsidize private charter schools ... especially religious oriented ones?"

"How about the age-old question of funding basic education."

Higher education also drew significant attention. Readers questioned proposals to consolidate colleges and universities and worried about brain drain.

"What is there answer to making higher ed more affordable & stemming the brain drain tide?"

$Democracy, voting rights and redistricting

Readers repeatedly said Ohio voters are frustrated that politicians often ignore what voters approve at the ballot box.

That made voting rights, redistricting and election integrity one of the strongest themes.

Many asked whether candidates would protect ballot drop boxes, vote-by-mail access, automatic voter registration and independent redistricting reforms.

Others specifically referenced past votes by citizens on abortion rights and marijuana legalization, saying elected leaders should stop trying to rewrite outcomes voters already decided.

"How about their positions on respecting the will of the voters?"

"Ensuring the ability of all eligible voters to cast ballots is key."

"Emphasis on redistricting."

This issue crossed ideological lines more than many others, with voters simply wanting rules they trust.

$Taxes, affordability and housing

A large share of readers focused on the cost of living.

They asked about property taxes, income taxes, senior tax relief, housing affordability and whether Ohio's tax system unfairly burdens working people and retirees.

"Reducing real estate tax for seniors."

"Housing affordability and supply."

"Most of all, how will they pay for all their promises?"

These readers want less rhetoric and more math.

$Healthcare, Medicaid and childcare

Healthcare ranked high, especially among readers worried about Medicaid, mental health access, senior services and support for low-income Ohioans.

Others asked about daycare affordability and maternal health.

"What are they doing for the healthcare of Ohioans?"

"Medicaid funding, SNAP."

"Access to healthcare!"

Medical aid in dying also surfaced repeatedly, showing how end-of-life policy is gaining public attention. A new bill was announced last week to legalize it in Ohio.

$Guns, crime and public safety

Many readers asked for clarity on firearms laws, red-flag proposals, safe storage rules, gun buybacks and local control over gun regulation.

Others cited youth suicide, school shootings and urban violence.

"How about gun reform...?"

"Secure storage of firearms to protect children."

"Allowing each city above 25,000 population to establish laws pertaining to guns."

Trump, independence and character

Though not always framed as policy, many responses centered on whether the next governor would be independent of national political figures or simply serve party interests.

Numerous readers asked variations on whether candidates would stand up to Donald Trump, resist pressure from special interests, or govern Ohio first.

Others asked about ethics, campaign money, corruption and utility influence in Columbus.

"Do you renounce Trump...?"

"How will she ENSURE us independence from special interests...?"

"How does Ohio remove the control of the utilities over the government?"

$And the issues raised by only a few -- but still worth asking

Some readers raised matters mentioned only once or twice, yet each reflects a pocket of real concern: AI regulation, sports gambling, vaccine policy, truck drivers speaking English, antisemitism, ICE detention centers, crisis pregnancy centers, National Guard deployment, cannabis rules, library funding, vacant housing reuse, STRS pension reform, social media fact-checking, campaign ad truthfulness, vocational education, organic farming, ranked-choice voting, juvenile justice reform and even traffic management on freeways.

That's the beauty of asking readers directly. They broaden the agenda beyond what campaigns choose to discuss. Readers asked the questions. Reporters from cleveland.com and The Plain Dealer plan to spend the next weeks and months working to get answers.

The Advance Local Express Desk uses AI tools to assist in producing content, which is reviewed and edited by staff.

Ohio voters already are weary of the same old same old tactics and messaging that is emerging in the Ohio race for governor. They want real answers to their questions, and hundreds offered their questions for Vivek Ramaswamy, below left, and Amy Acton, to the newsroom at cleveland.com and The Plain Dealer. Getty Images

value.Title: Report: Carbon taxes cost Pennsylvania $5B
value.Overview: The Center Square, ENVIRONMENT NEWS & CLIMATE CHANGE NEWS, 1245words

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JGF-P4N3-RXSN-X26G-00000-00Report: Carbon taxes cost Pennsylvania $5B2026-05-01T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:08:35ZLexisNexisENVIRONMENT NEWS & CLIMATE CHANGE NEWSReport: Carbon taxes cost Pennsylvania $5BBy Christen Smith | The Center Square

(The Center Square) - Energy is big business in Pennsylvania.

Its storied fossil fuel reserves have kept utility bills lower and the regional power grid more stable for the better part of a century.

That's remained key as states opt for lower-emission power sources, which have so far relied on fledgling technologies not yet able to keep the lights on with batteries, solar panels and wind turbines alone.

A new report from the Commonwealth Foundation concludes it could have been even bigger business had state regulators not spent the last six years "flirting" with a carbon tax program called the Regional Greenhouse Gas Initiative, the success of which many critics say relies on the fossil fuels it means to curb.

For the program's most ardent supporters, the transition away from coal and gas must start somewhere. And, to them, a 50% reduction in emissions and $6 billion in revenue over the last 18 years isn't a bad one.

"The Keystone State's six-year on-again, off-again relationship with RGGI is a cautionary tale for other states, such as Virginia," said Elizabeth Stelle, vice president of policy for the Commonwealth Foundation. "RGGI is a carbon tax in sheep's clothing, toppling the energy future of every state it touches."

RGGI, as the program is often referred to, profits from reductions in harmful emissions by charging generators for the air pollution they create, measured in carbon dioxide. Across 12 states in the mid-Atlantic and New England, power plants and industrial sites buy an ever-decreasing number of emissions "credits" at the program's annual auction to cover that anticipated pollution.

Those credit prices have risen sharply since RGGI's first auction in 2008, and because of the balance needed between fossil fuels and renewable power, the program has put financial pressure on the entire grid, leaving utility companies - and by extension, ratepayers - scrambling to make up the difference.

Sometimes relief comes from RGGI-funded bill-reduction programs administered by participating states. Or the proliferation of more efficient renewable sources, supported by government taxpayer-funded subsidies, that require less support from gas and coal. In recent years, it's been the pooling of smart thermostats, rooftop solar panels, and battery storage to compensate consumers for lower electricity usage.

And yes, it's been rate increases too. But if not for Pennsylvania, where 25% of the regional power grid's electricity is sourced, conventional wisdom has long held that those hikes could have been far steeper.

Which is why former two-term Democratic Gov. Tom Wolf's 2019 executive order adding Pennsylvania to the RGGI program triggered a six-year regulatory war with lawmakers to stop it before it ever happened.

The foundation, a conservative policy group based in Harrisburg, was among the loudest critics - behind legislative Republicans, with whom the organization typically aligns - of the proposal, citing quadrupled energy costs and a $5 billion investment shortfall, according to its report.

In terms of raw numbers, the chilling effect of potentially joining RGGI is stark. Between 2013 and 2018, 73% of proposed power projects in the commonwealth became operational. After regulators set their sights on RGGI in 2019, that conversion rate collapsed to 9%.

By comparison, the rate for Ohio - a non-RGGI state with a wealth of fossil fuel plants - declined much more slowly, from 62% to 48%, in part due to falling gas prices.

"Unfortunately, that loss is not shocking," Stelle said. "While Pennsylvania debated, litigated, and delayed over carbon tax policy, Ohio provided developers with a predictable regulatory environment to commit billions in capital. Projects that might have started in Pennsylvania went to Ohio or never came to fruition."

Losing to "friggin' Ohio" has jokingly been a thorn in the side of first-term Democratic Gov. Josh Shapiro since he took office in 2023, even calling out the Buckeye State's competitive economic policies during his first budget address to the General Assembly.

Shapiro, on the campaign trail, expressed his doubts about RGGI, though he continued the Wolf administration's legal fight to make Pennsylvania participate for two more years, only dropping it as part of a four-month-late budget deal this past November.

RGGI detractors say taxing generators would not just make it less profitable to build new power plants. It would raise energy costs for everyone by accelerating the closure of long-serving facilities, reducing supply, and raising electricity prices.

Shapiro, too, worries about affordability and instability, though he's pointed the finger at PJM, the organization that manages the power grid, and at its lengthy planning process. He even filed complaints with federal regulators to force the organization to lower electricity price caps, temporarily preventing a 30% spike for ratepayers.

He's even threatened, more than once, to decouple the state from the grid and "go it alone" - an unprecedented move that could undo a century of cooperation.

It hasn't come to that yet. Instead, Shapiro has lobbied the General Assembly to act on his state-centric Lightning Plan to quadruple wind and solar power over the decade - and still limit emissions from larger plants.

Stelle said doing so would double residential electricity bills.

"Affordable, reliable energy is not achieved through Green New Deal-style mandates and burdensome regulation," she said. "It happens naturally when we pursue policies that welcome the investment its grid desperately needs, letting Pennsylvania's rich energy sector do what it does best."

In the eyes of some, air pollution is what the energy industry does best. In 2020, federal data rankedPennsylvania fourth nationwide for carbon emissions, and estimates suggest that 1% of global greenhouse gas pollution comes from the commonwealth alone.

Supporters of programs like RGGI often point to the dual impacts of reduced emissions and investment in renewable technology as key to their value for an energy-rich state like Pennsylvania.

A 2023 report from the Kleinman Center for Energy Policy at the University of Pennsylvania concluded that RGGI participation would have reduced the state's electricity-sector emissions by 84% in 2030, compared to roughly 52% without it.

It could have been achieved without raising utility bills or decimating energy exports, according to the report. Researchers said wind and solar generation would have softened the impact of lost natural gas production and retail electricity prices will actually decline 0.6% by the time the RGGI cap zeroes out in 2040.

Shapiro's state-based plans - the Pennsylvania Reliable Energy Sustainability Standard and the Pennsylvania Climate Emissions Reduction Act, PRESS and PACER for short - similarly broaden the mix of energy providers so that the power grid isn't sustained primarily by fossil fuels.

This time, however, 70% of the money recovered from state-imposed emissions caps would support bill-reduction programs.

And, based on modeling from Synapse Energy Economics that was commissioned by the state Department of Environmental Protection last year, it would lower residential bills by $1 per month.

What's not entirely clear is how rapid data center development would collide with such policy, if it were to be implemented. More than 50 proposals are pending across the state, and their economic viability could falter if the state recommits to carbon taxing programs.

value.Title: Resident-led campaign fails to reverse Ohio county's ban on renewables

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JHD-NTH3-SF20-J4B8-00000-00Resident-led campaign fails to reverse Ohio county's ban on renewables2026-05-06T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:08:35ZLexisNexisResident-led campaign fails to reverse Ohio county's ban on renewables

The views expressed in any and all content distributed by Newstex and its re-distributors (collectively, the "Newstex Authoritative Content") are solely those of the respective author(s) and not necessarily the views of Newstex or its re-distributors. Stories from such authors are provided "AS IS," with no warranties, and confer no rights. The material and information provided in Newstex Authoritative Content are for general information only and should not, in any respect, be relied on as professional advice. Newstex Authoritative Content is not "read and approved" before it is posted. Accordingly, neither Newstex nor its re-distributors make any claims, promises or guarantees about the accuracy, completeness, or adequacy of the information contained therein or linked to from such content, nor do they take responsibility for any aspect of such content. The Newstex Authoritative Content shall be construed as author-based content and commentary. Accordingly, no warranties or other guarantees are offered as to the quality of the opinions, commentary or anything else appearing in such Newstex Authoritative Content. Newstex and its re-distributors expressly reserve the right to delete stories at its and their sole discretion.

Kathiann M. Kowalski

May 6th, 2026 ( Canary Media - Delivered by Newstex )

Residents in Richland County, Ohio, voted narrowly Tuesday to keep a ban on utility-scale solar and wind across much of the community - a setback for those who hoped the referendum could serve as a blueprint for overcoming local restrictions on renewables nationwide.

The vote was 53% to 47% in favor of keeping the prohibitions adopted last July for 11 of the county's 18 townships. Turnout was 30%, according to the election results.

The rural county's referendum drew widespread attention because it was a rare example of community members trying to use a ballot measure to counter state and local limits on siting wind and solar energy, which have mushroomed in the U.S. in recent years. In Ohio, more than three dozen counties restrict one or both types of energy, an authority granted by a 2021 law. Fossil fuel and nuclear projects are exempt from such bans and from other hurdles the statute erected for renewable energy. If Richland County had voted to overturn its ban, it would have been a first for Ohio.

This election result was a loss for the local group Richland County Citizens for Property Rights and Job Development, which first got the issue on the ballot and then reached out to voters. The group argued that reversing the ban would attract jobs and businesses to the area while protecting property owners' rights to lease land for the energy development of their choice.

'As a group of your Richland County neighbors, we made a commitment to stand up to our elected officials and forced a countywide conversation about property rights and government overreach,' said Morgan Carroll, a leader in the campaign against the ban. 'We did it with integrity, with passion, and with a deep love for our community. That's not nothing. That is everything.'

Campaigning in favor of the ban was Richland Farmland Preservation, a group that claimed the amount of land required for solar and was incompatible with preserving the area's agricultural character. Darrell Banks, a committee member for that effort who is also a Richland County Commissioner, said of Tuesday's results, 'I think this is an affirmation by the voters that their township trustees and county commissioners are aligned with the best interests of their communities, and we appreciate the support of Richland County very much.'

Richland Farmland Preservation's funders included organizations with ties to people and groups who have promoted the natural gas industry. Emails released last Friday show communications between Banks and a strategist for a political consulting firm that has done substantial work for The Empowerment Alliance, a dark-money group that promotes natural gas. Banks said that the strategist, Tom Whatman, is a 'family friend.'

The links 'should trouble every Richland County resident, regardless of how they voted,' said Bella Bogin, director of programs for Ohio Citizen Action, a statewide advocacy organization whose volunteers assisted Richland County Citizens for Property Rights and Job Development on the referendum. 'This community deserved a fair process, and the fight to bring that back to the county is not over.'

Despite the loss, Brian McPeek, another leader of the campaign to end the renewables ban, stressed that the margin by which it lost in the very Republican county was quite narrow. 'The big thing that we showed yesterday is that this was not a partisan issue.'

Link to the original story.

value.Title: DTE Energy Company ( NYSE : DTE ) continues to outperform American Electric Power Company, Inc. by 0.3% in the past month

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JK3-WT63-RSWV-8486-00000-00DTE Energy Company (NYSE:DTE) continues to outperform American Electric Power Company, Inc. by 0.3% in the past month2026-05-14T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:55:21ZLexisNexisDTE Energy Company (NYSE:DTE) continues to outperform American Electric Power Company, Inc. by 0.3% in the past month

NYSE STOCK REPORT

DTE Energy Company's (NYSE:DTE) share price is continuing to outperform that of American Electric Power Company, Inc. (NASDAQ:AEP). DTE Energy Company's return over the past month has been -5.1% and American Electric Power Company, Inc.'s return has been -5.4% over the same period.

Here is how DTE Energy Company and American Electric Power Company, Inc. compare:

Company Name1 Month %3 Month%YTD %1 yr %DTE Energy Company-5.1-1.311.010.5American Electric Power Company, Inc.-5.4-0.812.732.6

Company Profile

DTE Energy operates as a Detroit-based diversified energy enterprise involved in the development and management of energy-related businesses and services nationwide. The core business provides vital energy infrastructure, with its largest revenue sources driven by regulated utility operations. These utility operations include an electric company serving 2.3 million customers in Southeast Michigan and a natural gas company serving 1.4 million customers across the state. The fastest-growing segments feature major data center expansions and custom energy solutions, which drive incremental infrastructure investments. Furthermore, the company maintains non-utility energy businesses focused on renewable energy generation and energy marketing and trading. By accelerating the transition to cleaner energy generation, the firm continually delivers reliable and sustainable power to residential, commercial, and large industrial end markets across the region.

Company Profile

American Electric Power is primarily engaged in the generation, transmission, and distribution of electricity across a diversified service territory spanning the eastern and western United States. The company serves various retail and wholesale customers operating in both competitive and regulated end markets. Its largest revenue sources are the Vertically Integrated Utilities segment, contributing $462 million in earnings, and the Transmission and Distribution Utilities segment, generating $237 million. The fastest-growing areas include transmission assets under AEP Transmission Holdco, driven by new PJM and SPP project awards, and commercial and industrial sales volumes bolstered by expanding data-processing load additions in Ohio and Texas. Overall, the business benefits from constructive regulatory environments that support capital investments while effectively addressing the rapidly rising demand from large-load commercial end-use customers.

Company Profile

Brookfield Renewable Partners operates a globally diversified renewable power and sustainable solutions platform. The core business centers on operating hydroelectric, utility-scale solar, wind, distributed solar, and storage facilities, alongside leading global nuclear services and carbon capture investments. The hydroelectric operations represent the largest revenue source, generating the highest proportionate revenues on the back of strong pricing and generation within the essential Canadian and Colombian fleets. Conversely, the wind and solar segments stand as the fastest-growing sections, delivering robust expansion through active development, strategic acquisitions, and asset recycling initiatives. The entity primarily serves utility companies, large corporate off-takers, and industrial customers requiring stable, inflation-linked energy security. The company scales its broad portfolio to support sustained, long-term cash flow growth across multiple high-barrier global energy end markets.

Company Profile

American Electric Power is primarily engaged in the generation, transmission, and distribution of electricity across a diversified service territory spanning the eastern and western United States. The fastest-growing areas include transmission assets under AEP Transmission Holdco, driven by new PJM and SPP project awards, and commercial and industrial sales volumes bolstered by expanding data-processing load additions in Ohio and Texas. Overall, the business benefits from constructive regulatory environments that support capital investments while effectively addressing the rapidly rising demand from large-load commercial end-use customers.

value.Title: -AEP Names Andy Gurgol Vice President of Investor Relations

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJF-V4P3-S45G-J17W-00000-00-AEP Names Andy Gurgol Vice President of Investor Relations2026-05-11T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:08:35ZLexisNexis-AEP Names Andy Gurgol Vice President of Investor Relations

May 11, 2026

Release date- 08052026 - COLUMBUS, Ohio - American Electric Power (Nasdaq: AEP) has named Andy Gurgol vice president of Investor Relations, effective May 9. He will succeed Darcy Reese, who will retire at the end of the year. Gurgol will report to Trevor Mihalik, executive vice president and chief financial officer.



'I am thrilled that Andy joined the AEP team this year, bringing his experience in corporate development and strategy to the finance organization,' Mihalik said. 'This is an exciting time for AEP as we work to seize the extraordinary growth opportunities ahead of us, and I believe Andy will excel at communicating our vision for the future to the investor community.'

Gurgol joined AEP in January 2026 as managing director, Corporate Strategy and Development. Prior to joining AEP, he spent nearly 14 years working in the utility and energy infrastructure sectors. Gurgol worked at Sempra for nearly a decade, where he held progressive leadership roles, including director, Corporate Development and Strategy. In this role, he was responsible for MA and strategy development across the enterprise. Earlier in his career, Gurgol worked at NextEra Energy, leading economic and strategic analyses for over $ 1.5 billion in renewable energy investments. He began his career with FirstEnergy in its financial forecasting and analytics department.

In addition to his time working in the utility sector, Gurgol worked at the World Resources Institute, where he served as senior manager of Conservation Finance. In this role, he led initiatives in partnership with utilities, private sector companies, and federal and state agencies to deploy investments in environmental restoration projects that mitigate catastrophic wildfire risk, strengthen infrastructure and community resilience, and generate attractive financial returns.

Gurgol holds a finance degree from the University of Toledo.

'Communicating AEP's strategy to execute and deliver on the tremendous growth plans ahead will be critical as we invest $ 78 billion in our system through 2030,' Gurgol said. 'I look forward to meeting our investors and analysts over the next several months to begin building our relationships.'

Since 2020, Reese has led AEP's investor relations team, overseeing shareholder engagement, guiding the quarterly earnings narrative, and directing the company's annual meeting of shareholders.

After more than 35 years in finance and accounting, Reese plans to retire at the end of 2026. She will continue to lead AEP's investor relations efforts until that time.

'Darcy has been an outstanding advocate for AEP with our investor community,' Mihalik added. 'She has been an integral member of our finance team, and her contributions to AEP have helped us grow into the company we are today. We wish Darcy and her family the best when she embarks on her next chapter at the end of the year.'

ABOUT AEP

American Electric Power (Nasdaq: AEP) is committed to improving our customers' lives with reliable, affordable power. We plan to invest $ 78 billion from 2026 through 2030 to enhance service for customers and support the growing energy needs of our communities. Our nearly 18,000 employees operate and maintain the nation's largest electric transmission system with 40,000 line miles, along with more than 252,000 miles of distribution lines to deliver energy to 5.6 million customers in 11 states. AEP also is one of the nation's largest electricity producers with approximately 32,000 megawatts of diverse owned and contracted generating capacity. We are focused on safety and operational excellence, creating value for our stakeholders and bringing opportunity to our service territory through economic development and community engagement. Our family of companies includes AEP Ohio, AEPTexas, Appalachian Power (in Virginia, West Virginia and Tennessee), Indiana Michigan Power, Kentucky Power, Public Service Company of Oklahoma, and Southwestern Electric Power Company (in Arkansas, Louisiana, east Texas and the Texas Panhandle). AEP also owns AEP Energy, which provides innovative competitive energy solutions nationwide. AEP is headquartered in Columbus, Ohio.

For more information, visit aep.com.

[Editorial queries for this story should be sent to newswire@enpublishing.co.uk ]

value.Title: Institute for Energy Economics & Financial Analysis: Germany's Hydrogen Bet Risks Wasting Tens of Billions of Taxpayer Euros

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JHK-FKF3-SB2N-X23R-00000-00Institute for Energy Economics & Financial Analysis: Germany's Hydrogen Bet Risks Wasting Tens of Billions of Taxpayer Euros2026-05-07T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:08:35ZLexisNexisInstitute for Energy Economics & Financial Analysis: Germany's Hydrogen Bet Risks Wasting Tens of Billions of Taxpayer EurosTargeted News ServiceLAKEWOOD, Ohio

(TNSbrep) -- The Institute for Energy Economics and Financial Analysis issued the following news release:

* * *

Germany's hydrogen bet risks wasting tens of billions of taxpayer euros

Key Takeaways:

Germany's hydrogen demand is likely to fall short of official projections, risking costly overbuilding of infrastructure.

The financing model behind Germany's hydrogen network shifts costs to taxpayers when demand disappoints.

Failure to meet optimistic hydrogen demand projections could require around Euros45 billion in additional public funding -- roughly Euros1,000 per German taxpayer.

Policy is expanding to prop up faltering demand. A shift away from renewable-based hydrogen towards natural gas-based hydrogen with carbon capture could prolong Germany's exposure to volatile gas markets, threatening energy security and long-term industrial competitiveness.

*

(IEEFA) | Germany risks overbuilding its hydrogen network and wasting tens of billions of euros by relying on overoptimistic hydrogen demand projections, according to new research from the Institute for Energy Economics and Financial Analysis (IEEFA).

With Europe's most ambitious hydrogen strategy, Germany still plans an economy-wide role for the fuel. Yet government outlooks fail to fully consider how electrification and cheaper alternatives will limit hydrogen use across heating, power generation, industry and transport.

IEEFA expects Germany's 2045 hydrogen demand to be at or below the lower end of official scenario ranges.

"This matters because Germany is financing its hydrogen network on the assumption that demand will grow quickly enough for users to repay the costs over time. If that demand fails to materialise, taxpayers will be on the hook," said Alasdair Docherty, an IEEFA sustainable finance analyst and co-author of the report (https://ieefa.org/sites/default/files/2026-05/Rethinking%20Germany%E2%80%99s%20hydrogen-led%20transition_May%202026.pdf).

IEEFA estimates that a limited-demand scenario could leave German taxpayers liable for at least Euros34.7 billion in pipeline costs by 2055.

While pipeline costs make up most of the fiscal exposure, weak hydrogen demand would also increase spending on hydrogen-ready power plants and prolong reliance on liquefied natural gas terminals.

The difference between a rapid and limited hydrogen rollout amounts to Euros45 billion in additional public funding, or Euros1,000 per German taxpayer.

Blue hydrogen deepens gas dependence

With public finances tied to pipeline use, German policy has focused on sustaining hydrogen demand rather than treating weak uptake as a signal to scale back the network.

Germany's Hydrogen Acceleration Act, passed by parliament in February 2026, designates blue hydrogen -- produced from natural gas with carbon capture and storage -- as being in the "overriding public interest". This marks a significant shift from Germany's original strategy, which focused exclusively on green hydrogen produced from renewable energy. "

A pivot to blue hydrogen would add a network of costly carbon dioxide pipelines and re-entrench Germany's dependence on volatile global gas markets, threatening long-term energy security and industrial competitiveness," said Docherty.

"Blue hydrogen is an expensive way to sacrifice energy independence."

Limiting taxpayer spending

IEEFA recommends that Germany align hydrogen infrastructure development with confirmed demand and supply commitments.

Importing hydrogen derivatives for targeted industrial applications could greatly reduce the need for a large-scale domestic pipeline buildout, lowering system costs and limiting public spending.

Coordinating hydrogen investment with the phaseout of support for liquefied natural gas terminals would reduce the risk of underwriting two overlapping systems against the same demand uncertainty.

"It is better to recognise infrastructure risk early than to justify an oversized network by artificially propping up demand at indefinite taxpayer expense," said Docherty.

* * *

About IEEFA

The Institute for Energy Economics and Financial Analysis (IEEFA) examines issues related to energy markets, trends and policies. The Institute's mission is to accelerate the transition to a diverse, sustainable and profitable energy economy. www.ieefa.org

* * *

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JHM-DPW3-RXW9-W11G-00000-00DTE Energy Company's 10-Year Earnings Growth Outperforms American Electric Power Company, Inc.2026-05-07T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:55:21ZLexisNexisDTE Energy Company's 10-Year Earnings Growth Outperforms American Electric Power Company, Inc.

NYSE STOCK REPORT

DTE Energy Company's growth in EPS (earnings per share) has been 7.4% annualized over 10 years.

In comparison, American Electric Power Company, Inc.'s growth in EPS (earnings per share) has been 4.9% annualized over 10 years.

DTE Energy Company's last trading price was $142.44 which implies a Price to Earnings multiple of 22.1. With an EPS growth rate of 8.4 that implies a PEG ratio (price/earnings to growth) of 2.63 when using the EPS growth rate of the past 3 years (investors prefer using a 3-year expected growth rate instead but each investor will have a different perspective on future growth).

American Electric Power Company, Inc.'s last trading price was $132.56 which implies a Price to Earnings multiple of 19.8. With an EPS growth rate of 14.1 that implies a PEG ratio (price/earnings to growth) of 1.4 when using the EPS growth rate of the past 3 years (investors prefer using a 3-year expected growth rate instead but each investor will have a different perspective on future growth).

Company Profile

DTE Energy operates as a Detroit-based diversified energy enterprise involved in the development and management of energy-related businesses and services nationwide. The core business provides vital energy infrastructure, with its largest revenue sources driven by regulated utility operations. These utility operations include an electric company serving 2.3 million customers in Southeast Michigan and a natural gas company serving 1.4 million customers across the state. The fastest-growing segments feature major data center expansions and custom energy solutions, which drive incremental infrastructure investments. Furthermore, the company maintains non-utility energy businesses focused on renewable energy generation and energy marketing and trading. By accelerating the transition to cleaner energy generation, the firm continually delivers reliable and sustainable power to residential, commercial, and large industrial end markets across the region.

Company Profile

American Electric Power is primarily engaged in the generation, transmission, and distribution of electricity across a diversified service territory spanning the eastern and western United States. The company serves various retail and wholesale customers operating in both competitive and regulated end markets. Its largest revenue sources are the Vertically Integrated Utilities segment, contributing $462 million in earnings, and the Transmission and Distribution Utilities segment, generating $237 million. The fastest-growing areas include transmission assets under AEP Transmission Holdco, driven by new PJM and SPP project awards, and commercial and industrial sales volumes bolstered by expanding data-processing load additions in Ohio and Texas. Overall, the business benefits from constructive regulatory environments that support capital investments while effectively addressing the rapidly rising demand from large-load commercial end-use customers.

value.Title: New Renewable Energy Study Findings Have Been Reported by Investigators at Kenyon College (From Anti-reflexive Politicization To Anti-reflexive Policies: the Emergence of Renewable Exclusion Zones In United States Environmental Policy)

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JHD-V0K3-SH3F-P2VY-00000-00New Renewable Energy Study Findings Have Been Reported by Investigators at Kenyon College (From Anti-reflexive Politicization To Anti-reflexive Policies: the Emergence of Renewable Exclusion Zones In United States Environmental Policy)2026-05-06T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:08:35ZLexisNexisENERGY - RENEWABLE ENERGYNew Renewable Energy Study Findings Have Been Reported by Investigators at Kenyon College (From Anti-reflexive Politicization To Anti-reflexive Policies: the Emergence of Renewable Exclusion Zones In United States Environmental Policy)


2026 MAY 06 (NewsRx) -- By a News Reporter-Staff News Editor at Ecology Daily News -- Research findings on Energy - Renewable Energy are discussed in a new report. According to news reporting out of Gambier, Ohio, by NewsRx editors, research stated, "Renewable energy investment and development are necessary, if insufficient, parts of any climate policy agenda. Scaling up renewable energy projects to the level needed to impact greenhouse gas emissions, particularly wind and solar projects, takes up large amounts of space."


Our news journalists obtained a quote from the research from Kenyon College, "As such, the rural United States may be uniquely positioned to benefit from an energy transition because of its plethora of land well-suited to host large-scale solar and wind operations. But, what if local governments in rural areas enact renewable exclusion zones, functionally banning large-scale renewable energy production within county limits? In this short paper, we focus on the case of Ohio to discuss trends in state and local policy that restrict renewable development, and argue that this represents an emerging tendency in the anti-reflexive movement from politicization (i.e., the strategic denial and obstruction of climate science and policy) of climate change to anti-reflexive policies precluding climate mitigation."
According to the news editors, the research concluded: "We also highlight that many rural areas may represent a context of reception conducive for anti-reflexive messaging, an understated obstacle for green energy transitions."
This research has been peer-reviewed.
For more information on this research see: From Anti-reflexive Politicization To Anti-reflexive Policies: the Emergence of Renewable Exclusion Zones In United States Environmental Policy. Energy Research & Social Science, 2026;135. Energy Research & Social Science can be contacted at: Elsevier, Radarweg 29, 1043 Nx Amsterdam, Netherlands.
Our news journalists report that additional information may be obtained by contacting Nicholas Theis, Kenyon College, Dept. of Sociology, Gambier, OH 43022, United States. Additional authors for this research include Amanda Sikirica and Mauricio Betancourt.
The direct object identifier (DOI) for that additional information is: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.erss.2026.104648. This DOI is a link to an online electronic document that is either free or for purchase, and can be your direct source for a journal article and its citation.
Keywords for this news article include: Gambier, Ohio, United States, North and Central America, Energy, Environment, Oil & Gas, Renewable Energy, Kenyon College.
Our reports deliver fact-based news of research and discoveries from around the world. Copyright 2026, NewsRx LLC

Bill Yingling

May 5th, 2026 ( Pennsylvania Business Report - Delivered by Newstex )

Pennsylvania's turbulent foray into the Regional Greenhouse Gas Initiative reduced investment in the state's electricity generation, resulting in a loss of energy projects and billions of dollars in revenue, according to a new analysis from the Commonwealth Foundation.

'The Keystone State's six-year on-again, off-again relationship with RGGI is a cautionary tale for other states, such as Virginia. RGGI is a tax on every user of electricity, toppling the energy future of every state it touches,' said Elizabeth Stelle, vice president of policy for the Commonwealth Foundation.

'In the six years under RGGI, Pennsylvania lost between $5 billion and $8 billion in investment in our energy sector,' she said in a written statement on May 4. 'The commonwealth lost enough electricity to power the entire Pittsburgh metro area.'

The Regional Greenhouse Gas Initiative, or RGGI, is a multi-state cap-and-trade program among eastern states that sets regional limits on carbon dioxide emissions, requiring fossil fuel power producers to buy at auction allowances to cover their emissions.

Emissions targets are reduced over time, with the idea that the program gives power producers a financial incentive to invest in cleaner technology. Revenue from RGGI is intended to be used by states for efforts such as renewable power and energy efficiency.

Six years after attempting to enter the RGGI, Pennsylvania withdrew late last year after the courts ruled that the program represented an unconstitutional tax on producers and legislators voted to end the state's participation.

The issue of power plant development is significanttodayas increasingly tight power supplies are being blamed for increasing consumer electricity rates and anticipated use by data centers is forecast to drive up power demand even further while growth in supply struggles to keep pace.

The Commonwealth Foundation report said during Pennsylvania's effort, developers who had proposed billions of dollars in new power generation capacity during the Marcellus Shale natural gas boom stopped developing new projects, while few of the existing proposals reached operation.

The new report authored by Joshua Schubert, Energy Policy Analyst at the Commonwealth Foundation, says during 'Pennsylvania's RGGI Odyssey,' new generation proposals fell 38 percent and projects powered by natural gas 'collapsed by 65 percent.'

Natural gas is a considerably cleaner-burning fuel than coal or oil, and Pennsylvania has huge reserves.

At the same time, the list of projects in neighboring Ohio - which is not a participant in RGGI - increased.

'While Pennsylvania debated, litigated, and delayed over carbon tax policy, Ohio provided developers withapredictable regulatory environment to commit billions in capital,' Stelle said. 'Projects in Pennsylvania were cancelled as the project pipeline in Ohio boomed.'

Pennsylvania attempted to enter RGGI in 2019 with an executive order from Gov. Tom Wolf. Legislative Republicans and the industry fought the effort in court, saying it represented an unconstitutional tax.

The state's involvement ended in November 2025 when the legislature adopted a budget that extricated the state from the program. The Pennsylvania Supreme Court then in January dismissed the remaining appeals in the matter.

Pennsylvania Gov. Josh Shapiro advocates a state-level program to replace RGGI. The Commonwealth Foundation report, however, argues that it would cost Pennsylvania $157.2 billion and double residential electricity bills.

While Pennsylvania is leaving RGGI, Virginia is re-entering the program, a move that leaves the Commonwealth Foundation perplexed given the rising demand in northern Virginia.

'Pennsylvania's investment drought under RGGI should be a warning to other states as electricity demand surges,' Stelle said. 'Virginia's re-entry to the program under newly elected Gov. Abigail Spanberger boggles the mind as electricity demand in data center row soars.'

The post Report: Pennsylvania's RGGI experiment cost billions in energy investment, stalled power projects appeared first on Pennsylvania Business Report.

Fri Apr 24, 2026: Duke Energy Secures 20-Year License for Robinson Nuclear Plant

Duke Energy Corporation has received a significant boost with the U.S. Nuclear Regulatory Commission's (NRC) decision to grant a 20-year extension for the operating license of its Robinson Nuclear Plant. This renewal allows the facility to continue producing energy until the year 2050, a crucial development for the utility giant. The announcement was made on April 23rd, 2026, and signifies Duke Energy's commitment to providing stable and sustainable energy to the region.

The Robinson Nuclear Plant, located in Hartsville, South Carolina, plays a vital role in Duke Energy's operations, supplying electricity to approximately 570,000 households. This license extension not only secures the plant's operational future but also aligns with Duke Energy's broader strategy of maintaining a diverse energy portfolio. The company is focused on integrating nuclear, natural gas, and renewable energy sources to meet the growing energy demands of its 8 million customers across various states, including the Carolinas, Indiana, Florida, Ohio, and Kentucky.

Duke Energy operates within the regulated utilities sector, boasting a market capitalization of approximately $99.64 billion. The company is segmented into electric utilities and infrastructure, gas utilities and infrastructure, and commercial renewables. Currently, the company's P/E ratio stands at 20.26, which is close to its five-year median, indicating that the stock may be fairly valued in the current market context. Investors are encouraged to consider this valuation alongside other financial metrics when evaluating the stock's potential.

The GF Score(TradeMark) for Duke Energy is 77 out of 100, reflecting strong performance across key metrics that can lead to higher long-term returns. The company's profitability rank is notably strong at 7 out of 10, underscoring its operational efficiency. However, concerns regarding financial strength persist, with a rating of just 3 out of 10, suggesting potential issues related to debt levels and overall financial health. Investors should carefully weigh these factors when considering Duke Energy as part of their investment portfolio.

In recent months, insider trading activity has raised eyebrows, with insiders selling $5.6 million worth of shares and no recorded insider buying. This trend may lead to questions about insider confidence in the company's future performance. Overall, Duke Energy presents a mixed outlook for investors. While the recent license renewal for the Robinson Nuclear Plant strengthens its operational foundation, its financial challenges could impact long-term growth. Investors are encouraged to explore further insights about Duke Energy and its current market position.

Recent News 2 of 3, Source: HourlyBusinessNews, 271 words

Wed Apr 22, 2026: Duke Energy Honored with Arbor Day Foundation’s Tree Line USA Award for 20th Year

Duke Energy Corporation has received national recognition for its tree care practices for the 20th consecutive year. The Arbor Day Foundation named Duke Energy Florida a 'Tree Line USA' utility, citing the company's rigorous tree pruning standards, comprehensive employee training, and public education efforts aimed at reducing power outages and safeguarding electrical infrastructure.

On 22nd April 2026, the Arbor Day Foundation lauded Duke Energy for meeting industry standards for tree care, supporting tree planting initiatives, and engaging in annual arbor education programs. The designation reflects Duke Energy's commitment to sustainable practices, as well as its proactive approach to maintaining the health of urban trees in proximity to electrical lines.

One of Duke Energy's ongoing initiatives includes an annual free tree giveaway for customers through the Arbor Day Foundation's Energy-Saving Trees program. In 2026, the company distributed 1,000 trees to Florida customers, bringing the total number of free trees given away statewide to over 16,000 since 2017. These trees are shipped directly to customers with detailed planting instructions designed to encourage proper placement away from power lines.

Company officials noted that proper tree placement is essential for conserving energy and minimizing the risk of outages during storm and hurricane seasons. Duke Energy also conducts year-round vegetation maintenance near power lines and ramps up these efforts ahead of hurricane season, ensuring a reliable power supply for its customers.

Additionally, Duke Energy reminds customers not to trim trees near power lines themselves and encourages them to contact Sunshine 811 before digging near underground utilities. More information about responsible tree planting near electrical equipment can be found through Duke Energy's Right Tree Right Place program.

Recent News 3 of 3, Source: HourlyBusinessNews, 395 words

Wed Apr 22, 2026: Duke Energy Foundation Launches America250 Grants for South Carolina Green Spaces

GREENVILLE, S.C.– To strengthen communities across South Carolina as the nation approaches its 250th anniversary, Duke Energy Foundation is awarding $275,000 to nonprofit organizations through a first round of funding as part of its America250 initiative. This funding, announced on 22nd April 2026, celebrates Earth Day and aims to enhance local parks, tree planting efforts, and trail maintenance projects throughout the Palmetto State.

The grants, which form part of a total investment exceeding $550,000 in South Carolina, are aimed at revitalizing parks and shared green spaces, supporting tree planting, and litter prevention initiatives. "As we look back on our country's 250-year anniversary, it's an opportunity to reflect on our nation's history while working to support the next generation," said Tim Pearson, Duke Energy South Carolina president. "We are proud to partner with local organizations who can help care for the Palmetto State now, so future generations can enjoy its beauty."

Duke Energy Foundation has awarded a total of 11 grants in this first round of funding. Notable recipients include the South Carolina Wildlife Federation, Foothills Trail Conservancy, and the Upstate Greenways & Trails Alliance. Each organization is expected to utilize the funding to enhance local environments and foster community engagement in conservation efforts. For instance, Upstate Greenways & Trails Alliance plans to create a new trail that highlights the scenic beauty of Lake Keowee while educating visitors about the Revolutionary War history in the area.

The positive responses from grant recipients highlight the importance of such initiatives. Matt Flege, Executive Director of Upstate Greenways & Trails Alliance, expressed gratitude for the support, stating, "The America250 grant will allow us to create a new trail showcasing the beauty of Lake Keowee to South Carolina residents and visitors alike." Similarly, Susan H. Wild, Chair of Sumter Green, emphasized how the investment strengthens their commitment to beautifying the community, ensuring that efforts made today will benefit future generations.

These grants mark the first awards under Duke Energy Foundation's America250 initiative, which represents a commitment of over $1 million towards community-driven projects across the company's six-state service territories. Additional grant recipients will be announced later this spring, including initiatives focused on veterans' workforce pathways and enhancing access to history and civics education. Duke Energy Foundation continues to provide nearly $30 million annually in philanthropic support to the communities it serves, reflecting its dedication to environmental stewardship and community development.

SECTION 2 TODAY'S BULLISH SIGNALS

2.1 Uptrend

Today's Volatility:

The 5% discount to 12-month high of $134.49 against the 19% premium to 12-month low of $107.42 suggests the stock continues to push for new highs.

Price/Moving Average Price of 1.04:

- The Price/MAP 200 for Duke Energy Corporation is 1.04. Being higher than 1 is a bullish indicator. The stock is trading above both its MAPs and the 50-day MAP of $133.05 is higher than the 200-day MAP of $122.54, a second bullish indicator. The 200-day MAP has increased to $122.54, a third bullish indicator.

Past Quarter:

- In the last three months the stock has hit a new 52-week high twelve times, pointing to a significant uptrend.

2.2 Other Bullish Signals

- Return on Equity of 9.0% versus sector average of 5.8%.

MCap/Total Assets:

- Tobin's Q Ratio, defined as MCap divided by Total Assets, is 0.5. Compared with the rest of the market the stock is undervalued.

- Net profit margin has averaged 14.4% in the last 3 years. This is better than the sector average of 11.8% and suggests a high margin of safety.

- As per the Du Pont analysis, Return on Equity of 9.0% is better than sector average of 5.8%. This is computed as net profit margin of 15.2% times asset turnover [sales/assets] of 0.16 times leverage factor [total assets/shareholders' equity] of 3.6.

Source: Duke Energy Corporation's, 10-K filing dated February 27, 2025, available at the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission website: https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/17797/000132616025000072/duk-20241231.htm and Duke Energy Corporation's website: https://investors.duke-energy.com/overview/default.aspx. company (Duke Energy Corporation), Ticker(DUK)

SECTION 3 ONGOING BULLISH PARAMETERS

3.1 Rule of 40

The stock scores a favorable score exceeding 40 when using the Rule of 40 (Revenue Growth plus EBITDA margin). Y.o.y revenue growth of 4%, EBITDA margin is 36.2% and the sum of the two 40.7% needs to exceed 40%.

Source: Duke Energy Corporation's, 10-K filing dated February 27, 2025, available at the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission website: https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/17797/000132616025000072/duk-20241231.htm and Duke Energy Corporation's website: https://investors.duke-energy.com/overview/default.aspx. company (Duke Energy Corporation), Ticker(DUK)

3.2 Created Market Value [CMV] past 17 yrs of $78.6 billion

- Market Capitalization has increased by $80.7 billion from $18.4 billion to $99.1 billion in the last 17 years. This increase comprises cumulative retained earnings (RETE) of $2 billion and Created Market Value of $78.6 billion. The Created Market Value multiple, defined by the change in MCap for every $1 of retained earnings is exemplary at $39.68.

3.3 Satisfies four criteria of Benjamin Graham

- The P/E of 22.3 is 0.4 times the highest average P/E of 57.7 in the last five years. This is a value criterion, according to Benjamin Graham who described as a value criterion "A P/E ratio down to less than four-tenth of the highest average P/E ratio the stock attained in the most recent five years".

-""Earnings growth over the most recent ten years of 7% compounded - that is a doubling of earnings in a five-year period""; the earnings per share have more than doubled in the last 5 years.

-""Stability of growth in earnings over the last 5 years, defined as no more than two declines of 5% or greater in year-end earnings""; there have been 2 declines in earnings in the last 5 years.

Source: Duke Energy Corporation's, 10-K filing dated February 27, 2025, available at the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission website: https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/17797/000132616025000072/duk-20241231.htm and Duke Energy Corporation's website: https://investors.duke-energy.com/overview/default.aspx. company (Duke Energy Corporation), Ticker(DUK)

3.4 Satisfies 5 out of 9 criteria of Joseph Piotroski [pass mark 5]:

- Positive net income.

- Positive operating cashflow.

- Return on Assets improvement [from 2.4% to 2.5%].

- Improvement in current ratio.

- Improvement in asset turnover.
